source.title: Post-Movement Beta Activity in Sensorimotor Cortex Indexes Confidence in the Estimations from Internal Models

confidence_rationale: This paper provides nuanced evidence for MECH-090. On one hand, it shows that post-movement beta synchronization (PMBS) reflects internal model confidence rather than direct motor inhibition -- supporting the idea that beta carries information about internal states, not just output gating. On the other hand, the finding that PMBS amplitude modulates subsequent motor adaptation (higher PMBS = less exploratory adjustment) implies beta does influence downstream behavior through internal model evaluation, blurring the clean output-gating-only distinction of MECH-090. The paper supports the claim that beta carries information about internal model state but complicates the claim that beta exclusively gates output without affecting internal processing.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Post-movement beta synchronization (PMBS) in sensorimotor cortex indexes the confidence in feedforward estimations from internal models. Higher PMBS reflects higher confidence in internal model predictions; lower PMBS reflects higher estimation uncertainty. PMBS changes are driven by estimation uncertainty rather than motor error magnitude or direct motor inhibition.
mapping.ree_translation: MECH-090 claims beta gates E3-to-action-selection propagation, not E3 internal updating. Tan et al. show that beta amplitude after movement reflects evaluation of the internal forward model -- this is an internal computation, not output gating per se. High beta (high model confidence) correlates with maintaining current motor policy (less adaptation); low beta (low model confidence) correlates with exploratory updating. This partially supports MECH-090 (beta's behavioral effect is on output stability, not internal computation) but also complicates it (beta reflects internal model state, suggesting it is not purely an output gate but carries information about internal processing quality).
mapping.mapping_caveat: The paper studies post-movement beta rebound (PMBS) in cortical sensorimotor areas, not pre-movement or sustained beta in BG/STN circuits during committed sequences. MECH-090 targets tonic beta during action sequences; PMBS is a phasic event after movement completion. The mapping from cortical PMBS to BG-level committed-sequence beta requires inference across circuit levels and temporal scales.
mapping.source_context: Healthy young human subjects (n=15) performing visuomotor adaptation with joystick movements. EEG recordings from sensorimotor cortex. Bayesian computational modeling of internal model uncertainty. No direct BG recordings.

confidence_components.notes: High source quality -- well-designed adaptation study with computational modeling, published in J Neurosci. Mapping fidelity is moderate: PMBS is a phasic cortical phenomenon while MECH-090 concerns tonic BG beta during sequences. Transfer risk is moderate-to-high: healthy subjects (good) but cortical EEG rather than BG recordings, and post-movement rather than mid-sequence beta. The mixed direction reflects genuine tension -- beta carries internal model information (complicating pure output-gate claim) but its behavioral effect is on output stability (supporting the output-gating functional role).
